explanation:
Supabase is an open-source Firebase alternative built on PostgreSQL. Instead of proprietary NoSQL, you get a real relational database with Row Level Security, realtime subscriptions, and auto-generated REST APIs. The client library wraps PostgREST (for database access) and GoTrue (for auth) into a single SDK.

The key difference from Firebase: your data lives in PostgreSQL, which you can query with SQL, join across tables, and migrate away from Supabase at any time. There's no vendor lock-in — it's just Postgres with extras.
key_takeaway: Supabase gives you a real PostgreSQL database with a JavaScript client — use `.from().select()` for queries and `.rpc()` for custom PostgreSQL functions.
try_it: Create a `posts` table in the Supabase dashboard. Use the client to insert 3 posts, then query them with a filter and ordering.

explanation:
Row Level Security (RLS) is PostgreSQL's native access control — policies that filter which rows a user can see or modify. Unlike Firebase rules (a custom DSL), RLS policies are standard SQL expressions evaluated by the database engine. This means your security is enforced at the database level, regardless of how data is accessed.

RLS policies use `auth.uid()` to get the current user's ID (set by the Supabase client's JWT). You define separate policies for SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE. The key insight: RLS policies are WHERE clauses appended to every query automatically.
key_takeaway: RLS policies are SQL WHERE clauses appended to every query — they enforce security at the database level, so even direct SQL access respects them.
try_it: Create RLS policies for a `messages` table: users can read messages in channels they're members of, create messages as themselves, and delete only their own messages.

explanation:
Supabase Realtime streams database changes to connected clients via WebSocket. When a row is inserted, updated, or deleted, all subscribed clients receive the change instantly — no polling, no custom WebSocket server. It's built on PostgreSQL's logical replication, so changes are captured at the database level.

Three broadcast modes exist: Database Changes (listen to table changes), Broadcast (custom pub/sub for ephemeral data like cursor positions), and Presence (track who's online with state). Database Changes require RLS — users only receive changes for rows they can access.
key_takeaway: Supabase Realtime streams database changes via WebSocket — use `postgres_changes` for table updates, `broadcast` for ephemeral data, and `presence` for online status.
try_it: Subscribe to a `messages` table and build a live chat UI. Use `postgres_changes` with `event: "INSERT"` to append new messages in real-time.

explanation:
Supabase Auth handles sign-up, sign-in, and session management with multiple providers. Magic Link (passwordless email) sends a one-time link that authenticates the user — no password to remember or store. OAuth lets users sign in with Google, GitHub, Discord, and 20+ providers.

The auth flow: client calls Supabase, Supabase redirects to provider, provider redirects back with a code, Supabase exchanges it for tokens, and sets a session cookie. The `onAuthStateChange` listener fires on every state change, just like Firebase's `onAuthStateChanged`.
key_takeaway: Use `signInWithOtp` for passwordless auth, `signInWithOAuth` for social logins, and `onAuthStateChange` as your single source of truth for auth state.
try_it: Implement Google OAuth sign-in. After sign-in, store the user's profile in a `profiles` table using a database trigger on `auth.users`.

explanation:
Supabase Edge Functions are Deno-based serverless functions that run close to your users. They're like Cloudflare Workers — lightweight, fast cold starts, and perfect for API proxies, webhooks, and server-side logic. Unlike Cloud Functions (Node.js), Edge Functions use Deno and TypeScript by default.

Use Edge Functions when you need server-side code: calling third-party APIs with secret keys, processing payments, generating PDFs, or running complex queries that shouldn't expose your schema to the client.
key_takeaway: Edge Functions are Deno-based serverless functions — use them for API proxies, webhooks, and operations requiring secret keys. Invoke them from the client with `supabase.functions.invoke()`.
try_it: Create an Edge Function that takes a user's email, checks if they exist in the `profiles` table, and returns their subscription status. Deploy and test it.

explanation:
Supabase Storage is an S3-compatible object store for files (images, videos, documents). Files are organized into buckets — public buckets serve files directly via CDN, while private buckets require authentication. Like tables, buckets have RLS policies that control who can upload, download, and delete files.

The upload flow: client gets a signed URL (temporary permission), uploads directly to storage, and the URL is stored in your database. This avoids routing large files through your server.
key_takeaway: Supabase Storage uses S3-compatible buckets with RLS policies — use `upload()` for files, `getPublicUrl()` for public files, and `createSignedUrl()` for private files.
try_it: Create a private `documents` bucket. Write a function that uploads a PDF, generates a signed URL valid for 24 hours, and stores the path in a `user_documents` table.

explanation:
Supabase practice focuses on mastering PostgreSQL-backed serverless patterns: RLS policies for row-level security, realtime subscriptions for live data, and Edge Functions for server-side logic. The key problem-solving pattern is thinking in terms of database-first security — define who can access what at the SQL level, then build your client code on top.

For storage and file handling, the patterns revolve around signed URLs for temporary access, bucket policies for upload/download permissions, and organizing files with user-scoped paths. Master these patterns and you can build any full-stack application with Supabase.
key_takeaway: Build Supabase apps database-first: define RLS policies in SQL, then use the client SDK to interact within those constraints.
try_it: Complete all 5 practice problems before moving on — each tests a different Supabase pattern.

practice_challenges.title: Build a Realtime Chat with Presence
practice_challenges.hint: Use postgres_changes with event INSERT on a messages table, then add a presence channel to track online users.
practice_challenges.description: Create a chat application where messages appear in real-time using Supabase Realtime subscriptions, and display a list of currently online users using the Presence API.
practice_challenges.example_input: Two browser tabs open, User A sends "Hello" in tab 1
practice_challenges.example_output: Tab 2 instantly shows "Hello" with User A listed as online

practice_challenges.title: RLS Policy for Multi-Tenant Workspace
practice_challenges.hint: Create a workspace_members junction table and use EXISTS subqueries in RLS policies to check membership.
practice_challenges.description: Design RLS policies for a workspace system where users can only see and modify data belonging to workspaces they are members of.
practice_challenges.example_input: User Alice is a member of workspace "Acme Corp" but not "Other Corp"
practice_challenges.example_output: Alice can query projects where workspace_id matches Acme Corp, but gets zero results for Other Corp

practice_challenges.title: Edge Function: Image Resize on Upload
practice_challenges.hint: Use a storage webhook trigger or call the Edge Function from the client after upload, then use the Supabase storage API to store resized versions.
practice_challenges.description: Create a Deno-based Edge Function that receives an uploaded image, resizes it to thumbnail (150px) and medium (600px) variants, and stores them back in a different storage bucket.
practice_challenges.example_input: Upload a 2000x1500 photo.jpg to the "uploads" bucket
practice_challenges.example_output: Three files stored: uploads/photo.jpg (original), thumbnails/photo_thumb.jpg (150px), medium/photo_med.jpg (600px)

practice_challenges.title: Database-Driven Auth with Profile Sync
practice_challenges.hint: Use a PostgreSQL trigger on auth.users that INSERTs into a profiles table, and an Edge Function for post-signup logic.
practice_challenges.description: Implement automatic profile creation on signup using a database trigger, OAuth metadata extraction, and a dashboard that displays user profile data with avatar upload.
practice_challenges.example_input: User signs up with Google OAuth (name: "Alice", email: "alice@gmail.com")
practice_challenges.example_output: profiles table auto-populated: {id, name: "Alice", email: "alice@gmail.com", avatar_url: "<google_photo>", created_at: now()}

practice_challenges.title: Full-Stack File Manager with Signed URLs
practice_challenges.hint: Combine bucket-level RLS policies, signed URL generation with expiry, and an Edge Function that logs all file access for audit.
practice_challenges.description: Build a secure file manager where users upload files to private buckets, share them via time-limited signed URLs, and all access is logged via an Edge Function audit trail.
practice_challenges.example_input: User uploads report.pdf, shares a signed URL valid for 24 hours with a colleague
practice_challenges.example_output: Colleague can access the file for 24 hours, audit log records: {file: report.pdf, accessed_by: colleague_id, timestamp, method: signed_url}
